speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five of the interview with Magda Blau. Magda, you were talking about for your punishment being sent to the experimental--

speaker: interviewee
text: The experiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: --experimental center. What sort of experiments were they doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, they did sterilization. And then they made different operation on woman, like taking out the woman business that just from sadistic way. I found-- I remember the Dr. Dering. He was a Polish doctor, but a very sadistic man. Imagine making those unnecessary operations for young women without anesthetic, without any medication. It was terrible. The girls cried and screamed, and he just did it. There was a doctor, Dr. Samuel, which was a very famous doctor. He said that the sterilization, what Dr. Clauberg is doing, they are not so dangerous, because Dr. Clauberg stealed his ideas, because he did work on this idea, and he didn't finish it. So even he, he sterilize the women, but they still will be able to have children. Because as he didn't finish that, so that is not so dangerous. More dangerous was the other operations what, what they did. And Dr. Samuel--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Dr. Samuel--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a Jewish doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not there at the clinic. He wasn't at the clinic.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to the clinic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Samuel?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Dr. Dering too, from the men's camp. But Dr. Samuel, he did reconstructing or healing if something was wrong. He didn't want to do unnecessary like Dering did. But he said that if I one day don't come back, that means they found out that I didn't do the operation what they wanted. And that's what happened. One day he didn't came back. So we knew that either-- that Dering put him in or the SS man put him in. They did such terrible operation that there was a French woman--

speaker: interviewer
text: All Jewish women?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. French woman. Adelaide Hautval was her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of the women there, how many would have been Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: From what?

speaker: interviewer
text: From the women they were experimenting on.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was hundreds of women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all Jewish? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the women the experiment done was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They was Belgish. A lot of them they been Greek and Belgish. Some Polish, but mostly Belgian and Greek women.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- but all Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you're telling a story.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and this woman, she was assigned to the experimental block. But she-- from beginning she said, I am not going to do the operations which are not necessary, and not without anesthetic, and not without healing, because when I did my doctor diploma, I promised to do my utmost best to help. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said-- sorry. I'll, I'll go back. Her name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adelaide Hautval.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was from where?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was from France.

speaker: interviewer
text: France.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came with a transport with French political women who'd been in d'Arcy. It was a prison, and from there, there came about 300 of them to Birkenau. At one stage, I was assigned by Katka to be their blockalteste. So we knew each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were the blockalteste for the-- for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Experimental block.

speaker: interviewer
text: Experimental block.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sent by punishment there because they knew that is a closed up. You can't go out. It's limited.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were the blockalteste. What was your job? What did you have to do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I-- I had to look after everything there. I also-- from beginning, I assigned the people to the doctors, because someone said that Clauberg is not so dangerous. So I'm-- where, where I could, I put as much patient as possible to Clauberg, because that wasn't so dangerous, it wasn't so painful, and like I said, it didn't have symptoms for the future in case they survived the war and can have children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what other work did you do there? You said you organized which doctors. What other things did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Arranged doctors, and about the food, the rations, because they needed bread and, and, and-- so this this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you organize the rations also for the doctors, or did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you organize the rations for the doctors, or did they go out of the clinic?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went out. They went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at nighttime, who was in the clinic?

speaker: interviewee
text: All those women, block plus the staff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the staff? Where were they from?

speaker: interviewee
text: They I brought with me. When they said I am going for punishment to-- sent to the new Barrack 10, so I said, but I need 16 girls. [LAUGHS] always had my-- all right. So I picked myself 16 girls who was working outside work, because here at least I knew that is inside work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's-- that was the start. And I also arranged-- because we been in the evening, and I set to, to a little bit enlighten the girls who wasn't so bad or who wasn't yet had experiments. So I arranged them to have like a theater. But I didn't last there long, because they made experiment on faces. They wanted to claim that the Jewish brain is not as developed like the German brain, so they made masks of the faces. And then they put the 20, 30 women it was each to one room and they gave me the key, because at night, 12 o'clock, they supposed to go to the-- train or tram? Train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Train to Germany. And when they been in the room--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why, why were they being taken to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there was a doctor had here-- there his laboratorium, or whatever, ever he did the experiments in this camp, in another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what sort of experiments? They wanted to make experiments on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But the-- one woman who was working there, like a nurse, her name was Mila Portashinski, she was intelligent girl. And he said, Magda, they-- how can they make-- find out that? They must kill us first and open the brain, and then find out. So it's not a sanatorium, what he promised. It will be all finished at crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she knocked on the window. Let me out! And I let her out. So another woman says please let me out. I have my sister here. So I let her out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that it won't be so easy, but I thought, I-- they know that they are going to death, and I just opened the door. So their death or my death-- I let them out. I take the risk. When they arrived to the train, two was missing. So that started the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So that happens. They, they wanted to kill me or to hang me or to shoot me, because this is sabotage. How dare I let the girls out? And next to the Barrack 10 was Barrack 11, and this was where they hang or shoot or killed or whatever the [INAUDIBLE] or any enemies to the Germans. And there as a hangman was a Jewish wrestler by the name [? Jakov. ?] And he sent me a message that he will do it quick. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I go through-- away. But what was-- one day, a, a woman came to pick me up to take me to a commander. His name was [GERMAN]. He usually made pom-- pulp out of a person who came in. Nobody came there alive out. So this girl, she hugged me, kissed me, because I never will see you again. When I came in, he said, what did you do? I said, I did what I thought is the best. And he asked me 10 times, and I 10 times asked him. And he didn't do anything to me. And then he said, all right. Out with you! Why do you think he let you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was thinking how, but going backwards, the Greek girls who was there with the experiments, they been so grateful because I helped where I could before the operation or after operation. So they made me-- I don't know from-- how they got the material-- two beautiful pillows and they put it on my bed. What do I do with two beautiful pillows? So when Hauptsturmfuhrer Dr. Wirths came in and he left the door open, and the car was there outside. So I ran with the pillows and I threw it in-- in the car. Was beautiful handmade pillows. He didn't say then nothing. When I came back from the commander alive, Dr. Wirths was standing there waiting for me, and he says, thank you for the pillows. So I knew that he did save me from being killed. But the women, they heard that I went very easy out of it, so they sent Irma Grese--

speaker: interviewer
text: The woman heard, or-- you said-- who, who thought you'd got out of it easily?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the people that thought you'd got out of it too easily?

speaker: interviewee
text: They checked what happened with me. Is she dead? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who, who checked?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the woman commander--

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: -- from Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mandel and Drexler. So when they found out that I am alive, nothing happened to me, and I will be happily living after, so they sent Irma Grese to bring me to the Birkenau. I ask her, Irma, tell me. Where are you taking me? And she said, you will see. They took me to Birkenau, and under the earth there was a little-- like a room, so small that four people could stand next to each other. There wasn't even room that you could sit or go down, because it was so tight that four women was next to each other. In the morning, I had to stand roll call. And after the roll call, the punishment was that I should wash all the toilets. When I arrived, very tired, suddenly a group of men came. I don't know who they are till now. I don't know how they found out. And they said, we heard what is your fate. So you lie down, and we will wash all the toilets for you. If somebody will come, one from us will watch, and then you quickly get up, and-- so that, that you are working. Quickly I got up. So I didn't know what to do. I sat on the toilet. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: SS man came, and he said, what are you going-- doing here? So I said, I am shitting, because the Germans are talking. But I didn't know he picked me to be the blockalteste for the elite-- like those who worked in the offices and this. So from the shit to the-- again to the-- Who picked you to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Blockfuhrer who was friend of the other SS man, who told him that I did the painting this time for the good of the SS too. So they been friends. And when he heard that-- what happened to me-- so he had a certain with the other-- they been friends-- to, to save me from it. Because seven days I couldn't manage. There-- so many toilets, and all night standing, then the roll call, then again the roll call.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to spend one night, in the end, standing. Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I was-- I was seven days standing, and I was seven day doing toilets. But not I did it-- the men did it. And the last day, he arrived and took me straightaway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you still had to go through the punishment first. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. I did do the punishment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were taken away. You were taken away and became blockalteste. What block was it? What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was not a block of-- like the Jewish women was. It was a wooden barrack, because they was mostly non-Jewish women who worked in the offices and in the establishment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it unusual for the-- for a Jew to be the supervisor over these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was. It was. And they were used to it, you know, to do-- like when it was the counting, they did all the businesses, because they been the [NON-ENGLISH], you know-- the high society. But when I came there, I said, look. Here-- he brought me there. And I said, when he went, I didn't ask to be your blockalteste. But one thing I ask you. I will do everything possible to help you, but you have to listen to me. If I say roll call, then drop everything and stand on the roll call, because it's not right that because you are office girls or what people should stand hours because the counting is not good. And also when I said that taking bread, then you should. Taking the food, you should. I can't do everything by myself, but I will do everything for you. And I did. The first day, you know, I went there where all the woods were, and I, I said that the SS man, Tauber, who took me to the barrack, he sent me here to exchange the blankets. We had trolleys, which we took with my staff. I had some-- about 16 girls who helped me. And I want new blankets. It's a order of his. He didn't order me, but they didn't go to check. And I-- and I came back, and we made the bed with the new blankets.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they came home and they saw, so they was-- you know? They were, like, flabbergasted. And so-- and they knew that when I say something, there that is. So I had it easy with them from that moment. So what year was it that you were in charge of this group?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '43. I don't know exactly which--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the hot part of the year? The cold part of the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me see. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Let's say middle of '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: Middle of '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I went--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hot?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was nice. It was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, because I know that we were standing outside. It was pleasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to that particular block? What number block was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The number I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know the number.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They, they was there, but I wasn't there too long either, because some others arrived. And then Katka put me there, you know, because she said you will be take care of them, if somebody will be there so he, he won't and you will save as much as you could.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- how long would you have spent looking after the clerical staff?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long would you have spent looking after those clerical workers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I got assignment to another barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long were you in that barrack, though, first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till some other people didn't arrive. It was sometimes more weeks or less weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Weeks, and the Dutch women arrived. And there I wasn't the blockalteste. I was just the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the top and the second.

speaker: interviewer
text: You reported to the blockalteste.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you report-- who did you report to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. The blockalteste was there, and I was her [NON-ENGLISH]. Like, I represented her. Or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Deputy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Deputy, yeah. I was her deputy. And I was-- because the Holland girls, they were such fine, soft-- like milk and honey--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jewish girls too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, yes. So I was always trying to, to help them and to tell them what and how to make life easier. But they was-- they was not for life. No. They couldn't take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know how they died? They came home, they put a finger to the top, and standing died. You saw this? You saw them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain a bit more. What does--

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, they came home and they couldn't take the hard work. I don't say all, but some of them, like, they decided they don't want to live. So they put a finger up, they looked up and like a statue, and they died. And, and some of them-- because they selected them out quickly. So they put them to a barrack, which was called 25, where they collected. If they had enough, then they didn't want to go with every. So they collected them. And it was-- the barrack was full, and also the backyard was full of them. And it was hot, hot, hot. So-- and I saw them poor girls outside and their faces swollen, and they been thirsty. So I came-- excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have a glass of water. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Have some-- a bit of-- have a bit of water. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So when I heard-- so I went there and I said, please let me in. Now, what you want here? I said, I'm going to give water to those girl. Are you crazy? They are going to the crematorium. I said, yes. You mind if I will give them? Go ahead. If you're crazy [INAUDIBLE]. So I went bucketful with a cup, and I went and I put there. And they were so grateful, because they were so dehydrated. And I patted their face a little bit with cold water, even though they are going to the gas chambers. But why to go so? So they said, no, you are crazy. We are not bothering, the others said. And who were these others? Were they Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were Jewish girls. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But they were so tough working there, you know. So it was so tough because they worked with it. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what, what happened then? Who did you work with after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, I went to work-- it was end of '33-- '43-- and I went to work to the office. I said, I have enough. I said--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- who did you say to you'd had enough?

speaker: interviewee
text: Katka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I had enough. I had all the nationality. I had Russian. I had-- I had one Russian village with children, babies, and, and that. They was very good. I was very good to them. Because the commander, when he picked me, so he said, you are a kindergarten teacher, so you will be looking after them. So I said, if I have to look after them, then I don't want them to be on the roll call. I want them to be able to be going to the sauna to be washed and to bathe, and I want them to have SS food. And I thought, no. He's going to give me a, a flap. And he said, you have-- you will have it all. When the Russian girls found out-- Russian women-- went out, because the women from-- till 30, they been in a different barrack. So they saw they are heaven, what I provided them. So they carried me all around that would take you to [RUSSIAN]. But anyway, in the future they did repay me many times what I did for them. Anyway, so I had the Russian, I had the Polish, I had a Czech, I had a French, I had the mix marriages with children, and then I had the barracks with-- with Dutch girls, the Belgium girls, what-- Polish girl. All. So I said--

speaker: interviewer
text: And these-- but these weren't all Jewish people. These-- or were they all Jewish people from--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. The, the Czech, the Polish, the Russian, they, they was all non-Jewish. French, yeah, non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there big groups of them or small groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only small group was the French, but the rest, there was thousands. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I said, enough. And I said, I want to a little bit-- so he said-- and then you will go to work to the [GERMAN], like-- what is a [GERMAN]? Like a clerical worker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Clerical worker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Clerical worker.
